story:
She was a kid, barely seventeen, cheeks blotched with shame, a pair of cuffs trembling in her hands. “I didn’t mean to,” she kept saying. “I didn’t think he’d get away.” I put an arm around her and let her lean into me, soft as if I were her mother. 

“It’s okay,” I told her, because that was what she needed to hear. But the world would know by morning that he was gone, and the footage would show who opened the door. My eyes flicked to the new red light on the hospital camera, one of the extra monitors I’d arranged for this wing. She hadn’t known it was there. When I told her, panic brightened her eyes. “I can’t go to prison,” she whispered.

“You’re not going to prison,” I said. “Not if I can help it.” But she couldn’t stay. He had enemies. Now she did, too. Anyone looking for him would look first at the girl who had become both witness and weakness. “There’s a shelter, far from here. Safe. Ocean air. Lie low until he’s caught.” Four hundred kilometers sounded like exile to her. She wanted to pack. There wasn’t time. A car waited downstairs. Resources, a bed, a small TV. A place to keep her breathing and hidden.

She thanked me. Clung to me. Believed I was saving her. I told her to go, and she went, the cuffs clattering where she dropped them, her footsteps fading down the stairwell. The wind lifted goosebumps on my arms. “She’s gone,” I called.

A door creaked from the shadowed alcove beside the camera’s blind spot. He emerged slowly—broad as a wall, neck veined, eyes shadowed, blue hospital gown gaping. The resemblance to her was there in the eyes, only harder. Prison had carved him up. The gentleness in his question—“She’s safe?”—sounded borrowed, like he wasn’t sure how to hold it.

“She will be,” I said.

“You scared her,” he said.

“She needed to be.” I had to be sure she stayed away and kept quiet. That had been the point of the camera, too—leverage where muscle might fail.

He dressed in scavenged clothes stretched too tight. I told him it was his turn to pay a debt: I had enemies. I needed them handled—not killed, not chaos, just pressure, deterrence, the kind of shadow work that keeps knives sheathed. He bristled. “No. I’ll be sent back.”

He took a step forward, and I stopped him—one more step and he’d be in the camera’s field of view. He glanced up and froze. He hadn’t known it would be there. The noose tightened, and my hand was on the rope. “You could be sent back right now,” I said, patting my phone.

Four hundred kilometers away, she was already sitting cross-legged on the bed at the safe house, the little TV humming blue light across her knees. He thought of his sister—of the fear on her face, of the little TV at the safe house humming the version of safety I’d sold her. He understood that my help came with iron strings. He growled, then nodded. “Fine. What shoud I do?”

I didn’t pretend this was clean. I’d steered a girl into hiding and a man into choices he didn’t want, and I would live with that weight. But I had learned early that safety is built, not given. Mine never came without leverage. And tonight, I was the one doing the building.
